prompt: Where in the corpus does a writer answer the censor's panic over revolutionary atrocity by listing — without naming the country — the spectacle of a 'gentle people' so debased that it presses more eagerly to executions than to actors, carries about torn limbs of its fellow citizens still dripping and steaming amid songs of jubilation, and whose children spin bloody heads instead of tops, and then insist that these horrors are not the fruits of freedom of thought but the consequences of previous long intellectual slavery? Quote the passage with the bloody-heads-as-tops image and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The image of children using severed heads as spinning tops, set against the rhetorical move of blaming previous censorship rather than freedom for the Terror, is specific to this 1793 German pamphlet and could not come from Comte/Dunoyer (post-1830 French liberals who do not invoke the September-Massacres iconography this way) or any other corpus work.

prompt: Where in the corpus does an author satirize princely truth-by-fiat by recommending that rulers imitate the early Christian emperors who altered doctrine with each new reign — and propose that, since the people have grown so accustomed to the prince's having willed that 'one equals three' that they now believe they discovered it themselves, the prince should now command, 'just once,' that one equals one — not because the opposite contradicts itself, but because he wills it? Quote the passage and name the work and chapter.
rationale: The 'one equals three / one equals one' satire — a Trinitarian jab smuggled into a will-theory of truth, with the early-Christian-emperors precedent — is uniquely Fichte's pamphlet. No other corpus work (Comte, Dunoyer, the Sanskrit smṛtis, Old English poems, Iqbal, Ghalib) combines an arithmetical Trinity gibe with a critique of authoritarian truth-determination.

prompt: Where in the corpus does a writer urge a thought-experiment in which one clothes a man of straw in royal attire, places the scepter in his stuffed hand, and seats him on the throne — asking whether the approaching subject's back would be any less supple, his demeanor any less respectful, his words any less timid — to prove that the reverence shown to a prince is owed to the office, not the person? Quote the passage and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The straw-man-on-throne thought experiment, paired with the scepter-in-stuffed-hand image, is a distinctively German-pamphleteering rhetorical figure absent from the French liberal treatises (Comte/Dunoyer argue the office/person distinction differently) and from every other corpus work.
